subject: Criminal Law
lesson_title: Assisted Suicide and the Right to Die

questions.question: Suicide was decriminalised in Ireland by the Criminal Law (Suicide) Act 1993. What did s.2 of that Act provide?

options.A: Suicide was decriminalised, and assisting suicide was also made lawful
options.B: Suicide was decriminalised — it is no longer a criminal offence for a person to take their own life; however, s.2(2) expressly preserved the criminal liability of any person who aids, abets, counsels or procures the suicide or attempted suicide of another
options.C: Suicide was decriminalised and the Act created a right to medically assisted dying
options.D: Suicide was decriminalised as a criminal offence but remains a civil wrong

questions.explanation: Section 2 of the Criminal Law (Suicide) Act 1993 decriminalised suicide in Ireland — it is no longer an offence for a person to attempt to take their own life or to take their own life. Before 1993, suicide and attempted suicide were criminal offences (suicide was a crime at common law derived from canon law). Critically, s.2(2) expressly preserved criminal liability for those who aid, abet, counsel, or procure the suicide or attempted suicide of another person. Such assistance carries a maximum sentence of 14 years imprisonment. Ireland has thus separated the legal status of the person who takes their own life (no longer criminal) from those who assist (remains criminal). Option A incorrectly extends decriminalisation to assistance. Option C invents a right to assisted dying that was not created. Option D is wrong — suicide does not constitute a civil wrong.

questions.question: In Fleming v Ireland [2013] IESC, Marie Fleming (who had advanced MS) challenged the constitutionality of the criminal prohibition on assisted suicide. She argued the prohibition violated her personal autonomy rights under Art 40.3 of the Constitution. How did the Supreme Court rule?

options.A: The Supreme Court upheld her challenge and declared the prohibition on assisted suicide unconstitutional
options.B: The Supreme Court rejected her challenge; the prohibition on assisted suicide does not violate the Constitution because the State has a compelling interest in protecting vulnerable persons from abuse, and the legislature (not the courts) is the appropriate body to assess whether a safe and verifiable assisted dying regime could be introduced
options.C: The Supreme Court upheld the challenge but suspended its declaration to allow the Oireachtas time to legislate
options.D: The Supreme Court found the prohibition violated Art 8 ECHR but upheld it on Art 8(2) grounds

questions.explanation: In Fleming v Ireland [2013] IESC, the Supreme Court unanimously rejected Marie Fleming's constitutional challenge. The court held: (1) The Constitution does not confer a right to assisted suicide; (2) The prohibition on assisted suicide is not unconstitutional — the State has a legitimate and compelling interest in protecting vulnerable persons (particularly the elderly, disabled, and terminally ill) from pressure or abuse in end-of-life situations; (3) Any change to the law permitting assisted dying should be made by the Oireachtas, which has the democratic mandate to assess complex social, medical, and ethical questions of this kind — it is not for the courts to do so; (4) The current prohibition does not breach Arts 40.3, 40.1, or 41 of the Constitution. The case was also taken to the ECtHR (Fleming v Ireland [2018]) where it was declared inadmissible after Fleming's death. Options A and C overstate the court's ruling. Option D incorrectly identifies Art 8 ECHR as the basis.

questions.question: In Pretty v United Kingdom [2002] ECtHR, Diane Pretty (who had motor neurone disease) argued that the UK's refusal to guarantee that her husband would not be prosecuted for assisting her death violated Art 8 ECHR (right to private life) and Art 3 ECHR (prohibition on inhuman treatment). How did the ECtHR rule?

options.A: The ECtHR upheld the challenge under Art 8 — the right to choose when and how to die is protected
options.B: The ECtHR rejected the challenge under Art 3 and found a violation of Art 8 but held it was justified under Art 8(2) as necessary for the protection of the rights of others (particularly vulnerable persons); the UK's assisted suicide prohibition was proportionate
options.C: The ECtHR upheld the challenge under Art 3 — forcing Diane Pretty to live in her condition constituted inhuman treatment
options.D: The ECtHR found no violation of any Convention right — end-of-life decisions are entirely within the State's margin of appreciation

questions.explanation: In Pretty v United Kingdom [2002] ECtHR, the court's analysis was nuanced: (1) Art 3 — the court rejected the claim that Art 3 required positive action by the state to enable assisted dying; suffering from a terminal illness does not, of itself, constitute 'inhuman or degrading treatment' attributable to the state; (2) Art 8 — the court found that the right to private life under Art 8 does encompass the right to choose how to live, including end-of-life decisions; however, the interference with Art 8 rights was justified under Art 8(2) because the prohibition on assisted suicide is 'necessary in a democratic society' for the protection of the rights and freedoms of others (particularly the vulnerable). The UK law was not disproportionate. This case is important for Irish law because Irish courts have followed similar reasoning. Option A states the outcome correctly (Art 8 engaged) but understates the Article 8(2) justification. Options C and D mischaracterise the court's conclusions.

questions.question: The Dying with Dignity Bill 2020 was a Private Members Bill introduced in the Irish Oireachtas. What did it propose, and what was its fate?

options.A: It proposed legalising all forms of euthanasia; it was enacted in 2021
options.B: It proposed a regulated system of assisted dying for terminally ill adults with full mental capacity; it passed second stage in the Dáil in October 2020 (a historic vote) but was subsequently referred to an Oireachtas committee for further examination and has not been enacted
options.C: It proposed criminalising all end-of-life care and was rejected at first stage
options.D: It proposed decriminalising voluntary euthanasia only (not assisted suicide) and was enacted in 2022

questions.explanation: The Dying with Dignity Bill 2020 (a Private Members Bill introduced by Gino Kenny TD) proposed a regulated framework for medically assisted dying for terminally ill adults with full decision-making capacity. In October 2020, the Bill passed its second stage in Dáil Éireann — a historic moment representing the first time a chamber of the Oireachtas had voted to advance assisted dying legislation. The Bill was then referred to a Joint Oireachtas Committee on Assisted Dying (established 2021) for detailed examination, consultation, and report. As of the current date, Ireland has not enacted assisted dying legislation; the Joint Committee reported in 2024 recommending a limited form of assisted dying for terminally ill adults. Any legislation requires further Oireachtas consideration and enactment. Option A overstates what was proposed and its fate. Option C mischaracterises the Bill entirely. Option D invents a separate voluntary euthanasia Bill.

questions.question: A medical professional administers lethal medication to a terminally ill patient who has expressly and repeatedly requested to die, with full decision-making capacity. The patient has provided advance directives confirming this wish. Under current Irish law, what is the legal position of the medical professional?

options.A: Lawful — a competent patient's explicit and repeated request provides consent, making the administration lawful
options.B: Criminal — this constitutes murder (if the professional intended to kill or cause serious injury under s.4(1) CJA 1964) or, if the intent was to relieve suffering and death was foreseen as a consequence, the prosecution would analyse whether the double-effect doctrine applies; voluntary euthanasia is not lawful in Ireland
options.C: Lawful — the doctrine of double effect permits any administration of medication to a terminal patient
options.D: Criminal — but only if the patient had not consented in writing

questions.explanation: Under current Irish law, voluntary euthanasia (administering a lethal substance with the intent to end a patient's life, even at their request) is not lawful. The patient's consent is no defence to murder or manslaughter — consent is not a defence to homicide under Irish law (R v Brown [1994]; DPP v Lawler [1994]). The medical professional who deliberately administers a lethal dose with the intention to kill satisfies the mens rea for murder under s.4(1) CJA 1964. The double-effect doctrine (Option C) may apply where the primary purpose is pain relief and death is a foreseen but unintended side effect — but it does not apply where the primary purpose is to cause death. The Criminal Law (Suicide) Act 1993, s.2(2) also creates liability for assisting suicide. In the absence of enacted assisted dying legislation, such conduct remains criminal. Options A, C, and D each identify grounds that are not defences to homicide under Irish law.

questions.question: In Re a Ward of Court [1995] IESC, the Supreme Court considered whether a medical team could withdraw artificial nutrition and hydration from a person in a 'near persistent vegetative state' who had expressed a prior wish not to be maintained artificially. What did the court decide?

options.A: The court refused the application — withdrawing nutrition is homicide regardless of the patient's wishes
options.B: The court authorised withdrawal of artificial nutrition and hydration; the right to life does not impose an obligation to maintain life at all costs; a patient's best interests, informed by their prior expressed wishes and their constitutional right to bodily integrity and privacy, can justify withdrawal of treatment causing death
options.C: The court decided the matter was for the Oireachtas and declined to rule
options.D: The court held withdrawal was lawful only because the patient was already clinically dead

questions.explanation: Re a Ward of Court [1995] IESC is the most significant Irish Supreme Court authority on end-of-life decision-making. A woman in a 'near persistent vegetative state' had previously expressed wishes not to be maintained artificially. The Supreme Court authorised withdrawal of artificial nutrition and hydration, holding: (1) The constitutional right to life (Art 40.3) does not impose an obligation on the State or medical practitioners to maintain life at all costs; (2) The right to bodily integrity (Art 40.3) and the right to privacy include the right to refuse treatment; (3) Where a patient lacks current capacity, courts can assess their 'best interests' informed by prior expressed wishes; (4) Withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment is distinguishable from the positive administration of a lethal substance (euthanasia). This case is a cornerstone of Irish medical law and the withdrawal-of-treatment doctrine. Options A, C, and D misstate the court's conclusions.

questions.question: How does the withdrawal of treatment (passive euthanasia) differ legally from the active administration of a lethal substance (active euthanasia) in Irish law?

options.A: There is no legal distinction — both are murder if death results
options.B: Withdrawal of treatment (at the patient's request or in their best interests where they lack capacity) may be lawful — it involves allowing a natural process to take its course; active administration of a lethal substance with intent to kill constitutes murder regardless of the patient's wishes
options.C: Active euthanasia is lawful where the patient consents; passive euthanasia requires court approval
options.D: Both are lawful in Ireland provided the patient or their legal guardian consents

questions.explanation: Irish law draws a fundamental legal distinction between (1) passive euthanasia (withdrawal or withholding of life-sustaining treatment, allowing a natural disease process to cause death) and (2) active euthanasia (positive administration of a lethal substance to cause death). Under Re a Ward of Court [1995] IESC, withdrawal of treatment at the patient's request (or in their best interests) may be lawful and does not constitute murder — the cause of death is the underlying disease or condition, not the withdrawal. Under Irish law, active euthanasia — even at the patient's express request and with their full consent — constitutes murder (s.4(1) CJA 1964), because consent of the victim is not a defence to homicide. This distinction (act/omission; active/passive) is ethically controversial but remains embedded in Irish criminal and medical law. Options A, C, and D misstate the current legal position.

questions.question: A person who provides information to a terminally ill friend about medication doses that could be lethal, knowing the friend intends to use the information to end their life, may be prosecuted under which provision of Irish law?

options.B: Section 2(2) of the Criminal Law (Suicide) Act 1993 — aiding, abetting, counselling or procuring the suicide or attempted suicide of another; maximum sentence 14 years
options.C: No offence — providing information is constitutionally protected free speech

questions.explanation: Section 2(2) of the Criminal Law (Suicide) Act 1993 provides that 'a person who aids, abets, counsels or procures the suicide of another, or an attempt by another to commit suicide, shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction on indictment to imprisonment for a term not exceeding fourteen years.' Providing information about lethal medication doses knowing the recipient intends to use the information to end their life constitutes counselling or procuring an attempted suicide. This is the primary criminal provision targeting assisted suicide conduct in Ireland. The prosecution would need to prove the provider knew of the other person's intention. Option A (murder) would apply if the person administered the medication directly; providing information/counselling is more appropriately charged under the 1993 Act. Option C incorrectly treats assistance in suicide as protected speech. Option D misidentifies the applicable offence.
